chapter_summary: Modern national languages and cultures are not natural occurrences but consciously constructed artifices created by central governments to foster linguistic homogeneity. These standardized languages are maintained through national education systems, which counteract the natural tendency of oral languages to diverge and change over time.

claims.text: The level of an individual's literacy depends upon the breadth of their acquaintance with a national culture.
claims.source_passage: History explains from a quite different vantage point why the level of one's literacy depends upon the breadth of one's acquaintance with a national culture.

claims.text: The connection between literacy and national culture is a general principle that holds true for all modern nations.
claims.source_passage: History also shows why the connection between literacy and national culture is a general principle of modern times, one that holds true for all modern nations.

claims.text: National languages were as consciously and politically constructed as the national borders that separate them.
claims.source_passage: French and Italian, as well as English and all the other national languages, were just as consciously and politically constructed as the national borders that separate them.

claims.text: Standardized national languages were fixed by language normalizers acting under the direction of central national governments.
claims.source_passage: These standardized national languages were fixed in essentially their present forms by seventeenth-, eighteenth-, and (in some countries) nineteenth-century language normalizers who made their decisions, more often than not, at the direction of a central national government.

claims.text: National languages and national borders are codependent artifices.
claims.source_passage: National languages and national borders are codependent artifices.

claims.text: Linguistic uniformity inside modern nations is a self-conscious political and educational arrangement rather than a matter of chance.
claims.source_passage: This pattern did not arise by chance; it is a self-conscious political and educational arrangement.

claims.text: National education systems sustain national languages by enforcing standards found in dictionaries, spelling books, and grammars.
claims.source_passage: Inside a national border, education helps to keep the national language stable by holding it to standards that are set forth in national dictionaries, spelling books, pronunciation guides, and grammars.

claims.text: Before the spread of 19th-century literacy, languages followed a universal law of constant change and geographic divergence.
claims.source_passage: Up to Sweet's time, languages had followed the universal law of constant change. Whenever people who spoke the same oral dialect divided from each other geographically, their languages also came to diverge.

claims.text: Before national standardization, linguistic borders were fluid and based on shifting dialectal intelligibility.
claims.source_passage: No linguistic lines were painted across the road; the shifting linguistic borders could be drawn differently, depending on which dialect was used as a base.

claims.text: Prior to widespread literacy, languages lacked the external standards necessary to remain stable over time and distance.
claims.source_passage: Before the spread of literacy in the nineteenth century, speakers had neither an external standard nor an internal gyroscope to keep their languages stable.

claims.text: Linguist Henry Sweet predicted that the English language would eventually split into mutually unintelligible dialects.
claims.source_passage: Sweet was one of the most knowledgeable linguists of his day, and his prediction was one that other scholars of the time would have agreed with.

claims.text: Modern English has remained remarkably stable and mutually intelligible across geographic regions despite historical predictions of divergence.
claims.source_passage: The modern English language has turned out to be far more stable than anyone in those days could have predicted.

claims.text: The monolingual nation-state developed in tandem with the social and economic organization of industrialism.
claims.source_passage: The monolingual nation as we know it developed alongside a new kind of social and economic organization — the industrial nation state.

claims.text: In agrarian societies, local oral dialects were sufficient because economic units were small and community positions were fixed.
claims.source_passage: In earlier, agrarian times, when economies were local, one's occupation and position in the community were fixed. Because the economic unit was confined to a small area, an oral dialect sufficed

claims.text: Industrial society requires wider communication, which in turn necessitates literacy and a common language.
claims.source_passage: Because of the continually changing occupations that were increasingly demanded by large industrial societies, people had to communicate with a wider economic and social community. Achieving wider communication required literacy and a common language.

claims.text: Modern nationhood requires a centralized authority and wider communication circles to implement laws.
claims.source_passage: At the same time, the political system had to become correspondingly bigger, requiring wider circles of communication to carry out laws and provide centralized authority.

claims.text: National education systems teaching literacy and common culture are the core mechanism of modern nationhood.
claims.source_passage: At the heart of modern nationhood is the teaching of literacy and a common culture through a national system of education.

claims.text: Industrial society's educational system is the most universally standardized ever because of the need for generic, transferable training.
claims.source_passage: Industrial society may by most criteria be the most highly specialized society ever; but its educational system is the least specialized, the most universally standardized that has ever existed

claims.text: Members of industrial society must be able to communicate via written, impersonal, context-free messages.
claims.source_passage: Its members . . . must be able to communicate by means of written, impersonal, context-free, to-whom-it-may-concern-type messages.

claims.text: Totalitarian nations demonstrate that universal education is a functional prerequisite of modernity, as they prioritize it even when ignoring other democratic ideals.
claims.source_passage: Totalitarian nations may pay only lip service to such ideals as free speech and free elections. But their guarantee of universal education is, as Gellner says, 'an ideal more honored in the observance than the breach.'

claims.text: Numerical, technical, and general sophistication are functional prerequisites for industrial society.
claims.source_passage: levels of numerical, technical and general sophistication are among [industrial society's] functional prerequisites.

claims.text: Effective communication in a modern society requires a shared and standardized linguistic medium and script.
claims.source_passage: Hence these communications must be in the same shared and standardized linguistic medium and script.

claims.text: The functional requirements of industrialization explain the development of standardized languages and modern national economies.
claims.source_passage: Gellner's analysis explains the underlying reasons for the development of standardized languages and modern national economies

claims.text: Universal systems of education are a universal feature of modern nations because of the weight of their social consequences.
claims.source_passage: enormously expensive systems of education are universal in the modern nations... The explanation lies in the weighty consequences that proceed from education in our society.

claims.text: The employability, dignity, and self-respect of individuals in modern society depend primarily on their education.
claims.source_passage: The employability, dignity, security, and self-respect of individuals, typically, and for the majority of men now hinges on their education

claims.text: Education confers identity on the modern individual.
claims.source_passage: A man's education is by far his most precious investment, and in effect confers his identity on him.

claims.text: Modern loyalty is directed toward a culture rather than a monarch, land, or faith.
claims.source_passage: Modern man is not loyal to a monarch, or a land, or a faith, whatever he may say, but to a culture . . .

claims.text: Only school-transmitted culture confers usability and dignity on industrial man, whereas folk-transmitted culture does not.
claims.source_passage: [This] school-transmitted culture, not a folk-transmitted one, alone confers usability and dignity and self-respect on industrial man.

claims.text: The development of a common language was driven by the practical needs of manufacturers and bureaucrats for distance communication.
claims.source_passage: the manufacturer of textile goods certainly realized that he had to communicate in writing with his distant shippers... The bureaucrat engaged in army raising or tax raising realized that he had to communicate with officials and citizens thoughout the land.

claims.text: Global economic relationships have caused national vocabularies to grow in scope.
claims.source_passage: Now that economic relationships are instantaneous and global, national vocabularies have grown still larger in scope.

claims.text: Modern national vocabulary consists of three distinct domains: international, transnational (language-specific), and national.
claims.source_passage: Our national vocabulary has three distinct domains. The first is international... Lying beyond the core is the sphere of vocabulary needed for literacy in English... every literate person today has to possess information and vocabulary that is special to his or her own country.

claims.text: The international core lexicon of modern education is taught in all national educational systems regardless of language.
claims.source_passage: Taught in all national educational systems, and not confined to any particular national language, it is the most broadly shared literate vocabulary in the world.

claims.text: Transnational cultural literacy in English is shared by literate people in all nations where English is spoken.
claims.source_passage: This broad knowledge belongs to transnational cultural literacy in English, a sphere that is shared by literate Britons, Indians, South Africans, Americans, and Australians.

claims.text: Traditional elements of English cultural literacy persist because they facilitate international exchange.
claims.source_passage: they are probably here to stay, because they form a useful basis for international exchanges in English.

claims.text: Literacy in a specific nation requires domain-specific knowledge unique to that country.
claims.source_passage: in addition to broadly shared, international spheres of knowledge, every literate person today has to possess information and vocabulary that is special to his or her own country.

claims.text: National dimensions of cultural literacy are typically standardized alongside the national language.
claims.source_passage: In the early days of development, this dimension of cultural literacy is usually standardized along with the standard version of the national language.

claims.text: Early 20th-century China provides a cautionary example of a nation failing due to a lack of language unification.
claims.source_passage: in the early years of the twentieth century China presented a cautionary picture of what can happen in the modern world to a populous nation that lacks language unification

claims.text: A nation must have language unification and standardization to function successfully as a modern industrial and economic unit.
claims.source_passage: In the absence of a standard tongue China was not able to function successfully as a modern industrial and economic unit.

claims.text: China's lack of oral language standardization hindered its development as a modern nation in the early twentieth century.
claims.source_passage: in the early years of the twentieth century China presented a cautionary picture of what can happen in the modern world to a populous nation that lacks language unification and standardization.

claims.text: Alphabetic writing systems facilitate language standardization by creating a stable connection between writing and speaking.
claims.source_passage: European languages use an alphabetic system of writing that connects writing and speaking. The written spellings are stable, and because of the alphabetic — that is, semiphonetic — system of writing, the standardized spellings have a definite connection with spoken sounds.

claims.text: Widespread schooling in spelling and pronunciation prevents the spoken sounds of a language from deviating significantly from the standard.
claims.source_passage: Widespread schooling in spelling and pronunciation keeps the sounds of the language from straying very far.

claims.text: The Chinese written symbol is not phonetic and therefore does not stabilize the spoken language across different dialects.
claims.source_passage: if you are able to read and write Chinese you will not necessarily be able to speak to another person who can read and write Chinese, for the Chinese written symbol is not phonetic.

claims.text: Effective standardization of spoken language is possible without an alphabetic script if a national education system enforces common standards.
claims.source_passage: the example of modern Japan shows that effective standardization of the spoken language is possible without alphabetic script, as long as a national system of education enforces common standards for pronunciation and grammar.

claims.text: The easiest way to stabilize a language is to fix a single dialectal norm, freeze its grammar, and standardize spellings and pronunciations.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, the easiest way to standardize and stabilize a language is to follow the European pattern: fix upon a single dialectal norm, freeze its grammar at a particular time, fix standard spellings, and fix pronunciations that are reasonably well represented by the spellings.

claims.text: Fixed national languages are deliberate constructs requiring self-conscious planning in national education.
claims.source_passage: Fixed national languages are deliberate constructs... illustrating the need for self-conscious planning in national education.

claims.text: The real function of language academies was to eliminate linguistic diversity to create a single standard, despite the use of 'purification' as an ideological metaphor.
claims.source_passage: the real job of the French Academy and other dictionary making groups elsewhere was to fix the usage, grammar, and spelling of the national language... and, in consequence, to eliminate all other dialects, spellings, and pronunciations in order to create a single standard language.

claims.text: Spanish is a deliberately constructed national language rather than a naturally occurring ethnic language.
claims.source_passage: the language now spoken by Hispanics in our country was developed by exactly this same deliberate process. Spanish is no more an "ethnic" language than French.

claims.text: The primary goal of national language academies and dictionary makers is to fix usage, grammar, and spelling to create a single standard language.
claims.source_passage: The goal of the French Academy and other dictionary making groups elsewhere was to fix the usage, grammar, and spelling of the national language. They were to establish one form of spelling and pronunciation as the norm... to eliminate all other dialects, spellings, and pronunciations in order to create a single standard language.

claims.text: The Real Academia de la Lengua established court Castilian as the permanent basis for Spanish instruction.
claims.source_passage: In practical fact, court Castilian was set up as the dialect base of the Spanish national language — and forever after the Castilian-based Diccionario of the academy has been the permanent basis for school instruction wherever Spanish is taught and spoken.

claims.text: English standardization was achieved through indirect intellectual influence rather than a formal national academy.
claims.source_passage: The standardization of English achieved the same result, but by more indirect means. Although England did not establish a national academy, prominent intellectuals in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries... agitated to purify and ascertain the language in dictionaries and grammars.

claims.text: Hugh Blair's book was as influential in fixing the cultural content of the English language as Samuel Johnson's dictionary was in fixing its formal linguistic forms.
claims.source_passage: The clarity and authority of his book made it nearly as influential in fixing the cultural content of the language as Johnson's dictionary was in fixing its forms.

claims.text: English spelling, grammar, and punctuation have remained remarkably stable since the mid-18th century.
claims.source_passage: With few exceptions since Johnson's day, English spelling, grammar, and punctuation, as well as the main features of usage, have remained stable.

claims.text: Language standardization is characterized by an initial arbitrariness similar to the creation of physical measurement standards.
claims.source_passage: The most striking characteristic of language standardizing is its initial arbitrariness, which is similar to that of setting up almost any common standard.

claims.text: The fact of having a common standard is more important for society than the intrinsic character of the standard itself.
claims.source_passage: The fact of a common standard is much more important than the intrinsic character of the standard chosen.

claims.text: The justifications provided for specific linguistic standard choices are primarily social tools for persuasion rather than rational necessities.
claims.source_passage: But beyond helping to give the decisions a socially persuasive force, little hinges on such justifications.

claims.text: Standard written languages must be constructed from currently accepted forms even if those forms lack inherent efficiency.
claims.source_passage: The choice of forms and conventions of word order and grammar must all be taken from currently accepted forms, whether or not these have any inherent symmetry or efficiency.

claims.text: Modern English indicates grammatical relationships between words primarily through relative position rather than inflections.
claims.source_passage: Modern English indicates the grammatical relationships between words by the relative positions of the words.

claims.text: Linguistic forms and conventions of word order and grammar must be adopted from currently accepted forms regardless of their inherent symmetry or efficiency.
claims.source_passage: forms and conventions of word order and grammar must all be taken from currently accepted forms, whether or not these have any inherent symmetry or efficiency.

claims.text: The process of language normalization requires choosing one form from several candidates and declaring it the 'correct' one.
claims.source_passage: One form must be chosen from among several candidates and declared to be the pure or correct one.

claims.text: Distinguishing the third-person singular in modern English with a special 's' ending (e.g., 'runs') is linguistically pointless.
claims.source_passage: But in modern English it is quite pointless to distinguish the person by a special ending. We don't say 'runs' to mean 'he runs,' as the Italians do.

claims.text: Oral dialect patterns like 'he run' are more rational and represent a more advanced stage of syntactic language development than standard written English.
claims.source_passage: That arrangement would not only be more rational, it would represent a more advanced stage in the development of a syntactic language, as the linguist Jespersen has argued.

claims.text: English would have naturally evolved to a regularized verb system if it had remained an exclusively oral language.
claims.source_passage: It is a pattern that English would surely have reached if it had remained an oral language and been allowed to evolve further.

claims.text: The standard conjugated forms of the verb 'to be' (am, are, is) are linguistically inefficient compared to oral dialect patterns like 'I be, you be, he be'.
claims.source_passage: Contrast this model, from the standpoint of simplicity and efficiency, with the more advanced pattern developed in many oral dialects of English: I be We be You be You be He/she/it be They be

claims.text: Arbitrarily fixed national standard linguistic patterns are highly unlikely to be replaced by more rational dialect patterns once established.
claims.source_passage: But for all its virtues, this superior pattern is highly unlikely to replace the arbitrarily fixed national standard.

claims.text: Early English language normalizers were illogical pedants who introduced spellings that did not match pronunciation to show Latin etymology.
claims.source_passage: they were also illogical pedants in the matter of spelling, having left us some very peculiar spellings that, on the evidence, were never sounded as the letters indicate.

claims.text: It is unwise to tamper with established, universally recognized spellings despite their intrinsic drawbacks.
claims.source_passage: But it would be even more unwise to tamper with established spellings that are now recognized by everyone in hundreds of thousands of books.

claims.text: National languages are essentially and fundamentally different from oral dialects.
claims.source_passage: These examples show that national languages are essentially different from oral dialects.

claims.text: The formation of a written national language is impossible without conscious normalization and the codification of rules by theoreticians and institutions.
claims.source_passage: the formation of a new type of written language is impossible without conscious normalization, without theoretical comprehension of the norm, and codification of definite rules of pronunciation, usage, and inflection.

claims.text: The formation of a written language cannot be separated from the activity of normative theoreticians, grammarians, and language academies.
claims.source_passage: One should not separate the formation of a written language from the activity of normative theoreticians, from the creation of normative grammars and first dictionaries, or from the activity of language societies, academies, etc.

claims.text: The written norm of a national language is never a simple codification of the dialect characteristics of a single region.
claims.source_passage: the written norm is never in fact the simple codification of a system of dialect characteristics of any one region.

claims.text: An oral dialect cannot be transposed directly into a standard written language.
claims.source_passage: An oral dialect cannot be transposed directly into a standard written language.

claims.text: Written languages (grapholects) must be adapted to anonymous situations where the writer and reader are unknown to each other.
claims.source_passage: written languages, which Haugen calls grapholects, must be adapted to anonymous situations in which the writer cannot be sure who the reader will be.

claims.text: Oral dialects lack the vocabulary and structural conventions necessary for modern economic, scientific, and administrative functions.
claims.source_passage: Oral dialects lack the vocabulary needed to serve the new economic, scientific, and administrative functions of the nation.

claims.text: National written languages are not purely artificial constructs like Esperanto; they are living organisms that evolve naturally once fixed.
claims.source_passage: The self-conscious, artificial element in national written languages shouldn't make us misconceive them as purely artificial constructs like Esperanto. They are living organisms.

claims.text: Standard languages are conservative regarding grammar and spelling but evolve rapidly in terms of vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: But these events are comparatively rare and occur with glacial slowness, because of the conservatism of writing. But standard languages are not conservative in evolving vocabulary.

claims.text: Every national language is a conscious construct that transcends any particular dialect, region, or social class.
claims.source_passage: every national language is a conscious construct that transcends any particular dialect, region, or social class.

claims.text: Nationalism creates culture by selecting and reinterpreting folk materials to create a literate, specialist-transmitted 'high culture'.
claims.source_passage: Gellner shows that nation builders use a patchwork of scholarly folk materials... in reality selected and reinterpreted by intellectuals to create a culture upon which the life of the nation can rest.

claims.text: The cultures nationalism claims to defend and revive are often its own inventions or modified versions of the past.
claims.source_passage: The cultures it claims to defend and revive are often its own inventions, or are modified out of all recognition.

claims.text: Nationalism does not replace alien high culture with local low culture, but with a newly invented local high culture.
claims.source_passage: it does not then replace it by the old local low culture; it revives, or invents, a local high (literate, specialist-transmitted) culture of its own

claims.text: The creation of a national culture is a necessary dimension of building a nation, mirroring the necessity of a national language.
claims.source_passage: But the need for a culture in building a nation is really just another dimension of the need for a language.

claims.text: Successful nationalism creates a literate, specialist-transmitted 'high culture' rather than simply adopting local folk cultures.
claims.source_passage: If the nationalism prospers it eliminates the alien high culture, but it does not then replace it by the old local low culture; it revives, or invents, a local high (literate, specialist-transmitted) culture of its own

claims.text: A nation's language and its culture are effectively interchangeable or nested within one another.
claims.source_passage: A nation's language can be regarded as a part of its culture, or conversely, its culture can be regarded as the totality of its language.

claims.text: Mature literacy in a nation requires learning a specific national vocabulary of historical and literary associations beyond basic grammar.
claims.source_passage: Americans need to learn not just the grammar of their language but also their national vocabulary. They need to learn not just the associations of such words as to run but also the associations of such terms as Teddy Roosevelt, DNA, and Hamlet.

claims.text: Fixing the vocabulary of a national culture is a deliberate act of standardization analogous to fixing grammar and spelling.
claims.source_passage: For nation builders, fixing the vocabulary of a national culture is analogous to fixing a standard grammar, spelling, and pronunciation.

claims.text: The selection of specific figures or legends for a national culture is often arbitrary and not historically inevitable.
claims.source_passage: When culture makers begin their task in the early days of a nation, they are limited... by the range of materials that history has made available to them, but the choices made from those possibilities are not always inevitable.

claims.text: National education systems use textbooks to achieve cultural uniformity across a nation's territory.
claims.source_passage: In addition to using dictionaries that transmit the national language to all parts of the nation, national systems of education use textbooks and readers that carry the national culture to outlying provinces.

claims.text: The United States and Britain achieved cultural uniformity through decentralized, provincial textbooks rather than central government mandates.
claims.source_passage: The British, like the Americans, used more subtle means for achieving the same uniformity. Textbooks were not prepared by the central government but by the provinces.

claims.text: Hugh Blair was a foundational figure in defining cultural literacy for the English-speaking world.
claims.source_passage: He was thus an early, perhaps the first, definer of cultural literacy for the English national language.

claims.text: Blair's Rhetoric functioned as a dictionary of cultural literacy specifically for those outside the English elite, such as Scots and Americans.
claims.source_passage: Blair created, in effect, a dictionary of cultural literacy for those who had not been born to English literate culture, for use by provincials like the Scots and colonials like the Americans.

claims.text: Hugh Blair was an early, perhaps the first, definer of cultural literacy for the English national language.
claims.source_passage: He was thus an early, perhaps the first, definer of cultural literacy for the English national language.

claims.text: The literate tradition rendered by Blair was a composite of English, Greek, Roman, and European origins.
claims.source_passage: The literate tradition that Blair thus rendered explicit... was by no means simply English in its origins. It contained material from Greece and Rome and from Europe.

claims.text: True cultural literacy requires conveying the specific attitudes and associations—the 'lore'—attached to shared items, not just the names themselves.
claims.source_passage: For each of these items, and for many more, Blair conveyed the attitudes and associations that make up the lore of the literate tradition.

claims.text: Blair's work became obsolete by the early twentieth century because it lacked the nineteenth-century elements and national dimension necessary for modern cultural literacy.
claims.source_passage: By the early twentieth century Blair was obsolete, not because of any defect in his presentation but because essential new elements not found in his book had come into literate culture during the nineteenth century... and because such a book would still have lacked the national dimension of cultural literacy.

claims.text: American schoolbooks from 1790 to 1900 exhibited an almost complete unanimity of values and emphases.
claims.source_passage: In a study of American school materials of the nineteenth century, Ruth Miller Elson found an almost complete unanimity of values and emphases in our schoolbooks from 1790 to 1900.

claims.text: Nineteenth-century American schoolbooks consistently promoted a contrast between virtuous Americans and corrupt Europeans to foster national identity.
claims.source_passage: They consistently contrasted virtuous and natural Americans with corrupt and decadent Europeans; they unanimously stressed love of country, love of God, obedience to parents, thrift, honesty, and hard work.

claims.text: The interchangeability of American schoolbooks in the nineteenth century served to indoctrinate young Americans with fierce national loyalty and pride.
claims.source_passage: In fact, as Elson has shown, the contents of American schoolbooks of the nineteenth century were so similar and interchangeable that their creators might seem to have participated in a conspiracy to indoctrinate young Americans with commonly shared attitudes, including a fierce national loyalty and pride.

claims.text: Noah Webster was a typical and important figure in the militant effort to use schoolbooks to build American national identity.
claims.source_passage: Among the militant and self-conscious writers of these schoolbooks, the jingoistic Noah Webster was as typical as he was important.

claims.text: Mason Weems contributed significantly to the American literate tradition by romanticizing national figures like George Washington to instill virtue and patriotism.
claims.source_passage: His chef-d'oeuvre and main contribution to the American tradition was his biography of George Washington, wherein could [be found the mythology and values of the new country].

claims.text: Nineteenth-century American schoolbooks were so similar and interchangeable that they appeared to be a coordinated effort to indoctrinate youth with shared national pride.
claims.source_passage: In fact, as Elson has shown, the contents of American schoolbooks of the nineteenth century were so similar and interchangeable that their creators might seem to have participated in a conspiracy to indoctrinate young Americans with commonly shared attitudes, including a fierce national loyalty and pride.

claims.text: Mason Weems's biography of George Washington was his primary contribution to the American tradition, specifically introducing the cherry tree legend.
claims.source_passage: His chef-d'oeuvre and main contribution to the American tradition was his biography of George Washington, wherein could be found the original legend of the cherry tree.

claims.text: The story of the cherry tree serves as a useful illustration of the processes by which American national culture was formed.
claims.source_passage: But the story of the cherry tree is a useful illustration of the way our national culture was formed.

claims.text: Mason Weems was not consciously attempting to build a national tradition, but rather assumed Washington was already a part of American lore.
claims.source_passage: His letters suggest that he was not consciously helping to form part of the tradition needed to weld Americans into a nation. He assumed that George Washington had already become part of American lore by virtue of the great offices he had held.

claims.text: Official praise and focus on statesmanship made George Washington an intimidating and distant figure that children could not identify with.
claims.source_passage: Weems felt that these outpourings of official praise had turned Washington into an intimidating, distant personage, because they presented his life entirely in terms of generalship and statesmanship. No ordinary boy or girl could identify with such a demigod.

claims.text: The American public in the early 19th century had an infinite appetite for hagiography regarding George Washington.
claims.source_passage: But since Americans had an infinite appetite for Washingtonian hagiography, and since no one had yet humanized Washington, Weems detected a market opportunity.

claims.text: National culture requires a 'domesticated Everyman' figure whose private virtues serve as a relatable model for the youth.
claims.source_passage: Weems deduced that the public needed a domesticated Everyman whose life would serve as a model for American youth.

claims.text: The most persistent elements of national lore survive because of their human universality rather than deliberate political engineering.
claims.source_passage: Characteristically, some of the most persistent elements of our national lore owe their longevity to human universality rather than conscious political design

claims.text: The cherry tree story persists because it appeals to the universal experiences of children feeling wayward and parents desiring to encourage truth-telling.
claims.source_passage: There could hardly be a more attractive tale than the one which portrays for the delight of children a parent who happily forgives a wayward child (for what child does not feel wayward?) and for parents a model who, in the most charming possible way, persuades young people to tell the truth.

claims.text: The survival of Weems's stories in American lore is specifically due to their inclusion in school textbooks rather than the popularity of his original book.
claims.source_passage: If this legend had been left to languish in Weems's book, it might have been forgotten. ... But with a sure instinct, the compilers of textbooks took up the Weems stories.

claims.text: McGuffey's inclusion of the cherry tree story in his Second Eclectic Reader ensured its place in permanent American lore.
claims.source_passage: McGuffey included a sterner version of the cherry-tree episode in his Second Eclectic Reader and thus assured it a place in many other readers, and in our permanent lore.

claims.text: Abraham Lincoln's self-education through a small, specific set of books—including Weems and the Bible—became part of the American national mythology.
claims.source_passage: This typical frontier education itself became part of American national mythology after Lincoln's assassination.

claims.text: The American national myth suggests that reading a few central books can produce virtue, patriotism, and prudence.
claims.source_passage: Americans continued an ideal of a peculiarly American education, in which the reading of a few central books could yield virtue, patriotism, and prudence.

claims.text: The folklore version of Lincoln personified the traditional American scheme of education (paideia) and helped transmit it to later generations.
claims.source_passage: For the Lincoln of folklore embodied what ordinary inarticulate Americans cherished as ideals. Put otherwise, if the Lincoln of actuality imbibed the American paideia [i.e., our traditional scheme of education], the Lincoln of folklore personified it, and in reflecting it back on education writ large, helped transmit it to successive generations of Americans.

claims.text: National traditions are initially in flux but become fixed in the national memory once recorded in books and transmitted via education.
claims.source_passage: In the early stages of a nation's life, its traditions are in flux. But with the passage of time, traditions that have been recorded in a nation's printed books and transmitted in its education system become fixed in the national memory.

claims.text: Important legends, names, and events become fixed by constant usage in the same manner as linguistic spellings.
claims.source_passage: Important legends, names, and events become fixed by constant usage, just as spellings do.

claims.text: Languages are linguistically progressive, meaning they tend to shorten frequently used words over time.
claims.source_passage: All languages are linguistically progressive. That is, they tend to shorten words that are used frequently. For instance, TV will probably slowly supplant television, and phone, telephone.

claims.text: The national vocabulary can occasionally be changed by common will to remove social harms like racism and sexism.
claims.source_passage: Occasionally it is possible to change our vocabulary by acts of common will, as we are changing it to remove racism and sexism in language.

claims.text: It is a false and damaging myth that national culture can be remade on a large scale by an act of common will.
claims.source_passage: But the occurrence, and more rarely the introduction, of specific change into our vocabulary has led some to believe, erroneously, that our culture can be remade on a large scale by an act of common will. This is a false and damaging myth.

claims.text: Rapid, large-scale change is impossible in the sphere of national culture.
claims.source_passage: Rapid, large-scale change is no more possible in the sphere of national culture than in the sphere of national language.

claims.text: Dropping biblical and legendary allusions from national culture is neither desirable nor practicable.
claims.source_passage: It is no more desirable or practicable to drop biblical and legendary allusions from our culture than to drop the letter s from the third person singular.

claims.text: Traditional materials of national culture can only be learned by all citizens if they are taught in schools.
claims.source_passage: The traditional materials of national culture can be learned by all citizens only if the materials are taught in a nation's schools.

claims.text: Schooling requires access to books that explain cultural allusions, such as dictionaries and indexes.
claims.source_passage: But to teach them, the schools must have access to books that explain them — dictionaries like Johnson's and indexes to cultural literacy like Blair's.

claims.text: Compendia of cultural literacy are socially progressive instruments because they provide outsiders with access to mainstream literate culture.
claims.source_passage: Because such compendia help outsiders enter mainstream literate culture, works like Blair's and Johnson's are socially progressive instruments, despite the fact that they contain traditional materials.

claims.text: Dictionaries of cultural literacy have historically helped to overcome class distinctions and barriers to opportunity.
claims.source_passage: Like everything that helps to spread literate language and culture, a nation's dictionaries, including those of cultural literacy, have helped to overcome class distinctions and barriers to opportunity. Historically, they have had a liberalizing and democratic effect.

claims.text: The United States benefited from inheriting a standard written language that was already normalized by 1776.
claims.source_passage: Fortunately, we inherited a standard written language that by 1776 had become normalized in grammar, spelling, and pronunciation.

claims.text: The stability of American political charters over 200 years is due to the normalization of the English language by the time they were written.
claims.source_passage: Our ancient charters, the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution, were written in a language that is current more than two hundred years later — a remarkable fact that we too easily take for granted when we read them.

claims.text: The United States did not experience the bloody animosities and social dislocations associated with the imposition of national languages in Europe because it inherited a pre-normalized language.
claims.source_passage: When our nation began, we did not experience the bloody animosities and social dislocations that followed the imposition of national languages in France, Spain, and Britain... Fortunately, we inherited a standard written language that by 1776 had become normalized in grammar, spelling, and pronunciation.

claims.text: The Declaration of Independence and the Constitution are written in a language that remains current and accessible more than two hundred years later.
claims.source_passage: Our ancient charters, the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution, were written in a language that is current more than two hundred years later — a remarkable fact that we too easily take for granted when we read them.

claims.text: The standardization of the English language was the result of historical military conflicts and the long-term efforts of scholars and teachers.
claims.source_passage: The work of standardizing our language had been done for us long ago in such bloody, faraway battles as Flodden, Worcester, and Drogheda and in numerous decades of work by English scholars and schoolmasters.

claims.text: The only significant internal conflict the U.S. had regarding language was the 'war of the dictionaries' between Noah Webster and Joseph Worcester.
claims.source_passage: Our only experience of civil strife over the language was a tempest in a teapot called the war of the dictionaries. Joseph Worcester of Massachusetts and Noah Webster of Connecticut argued over such towering questions as whether the a in grass should be pronounced like the 'grabs of a British lawn or the grass of the boundless prairies.'

claims.text: American equanimity toward multilingualism stems from a lack of historical experience with the bloodshed typically required to establish a national language.
claims.source_passage: Our fortunate inexperience of bloodshed over language may explain why some of us look with equanimity upon recent proposals favoring multilingualism in our country.

claims.text: Switzerland's success is a model of 'multiliteracy' rather than simple multilingualism, achieved through centralized education and military service.
claims.source_passage: Switzerland has achieved multiliteracy (as distinct from multilingualism) through a small, intensive, centralized educational system that, coupled with universal military service, enables the Swiss to communicate with one another despite their linguistic handicaps.

claims.text: Large nations have been unable to successfully imitate the Swiss model of multilingualism.
claims.source_passage: In fact, there are good reasons why no large nation has been able to imitate Switzerland. The examples of Belgium and Canada are not encouraging.

claims.text: The United States has failed to achieve effective monoliteracy, let alone multiliteracy.
claims.source_passage: In America the reality is that we have not yet properly achieved monoliteracy, much less multiliteracy.

claims.text: Modern technology creates an increased necessity for effective monoliteracy.
claims.source_passage: Because of the demands created by technology we need effective monoliteracy more than ever.

claims.text: Multilingualism increases cultural fragmentation, civil antagonism, illiteracy, and economic-technological ineffectualness.
claims.source_passage: But in fact, multilingualism enormously increases cultural fragmentation, civil antagonism, illiteracy, and economic-technological ineffectualness.

claims.text: National languages are artificial constructs created to overcome local and ethnic dialectal variations within a large nation.
claims.source_passage: National languages are not ethnic media. Each one is an elaborate composite contrived to overcome local and ethnic dialectal variations inside a large nation.

claims.text: Encouraging more than one national language within a modern nation's borders is contrary to the purpose and essence of a national language.
claims.source_passage: It is contrary to the purpose and essence of a national language, whether English or German or Spanish or French, that a modern nation should deliberately encourage more than one to flourish within its borders.

claims.text: Once a national language is standardized and fixed in media and education, it becomes an almost unchanging substance.
claims.source_passage: Once a national language is permanently fixed in grammars, schoolbooks, and dictionaries, and used in millions of books, magazines, and newspapers, it becomes, except for its vocabulary, an immovable, almost unchanging substance.

claims.text: Linguistic pluralism (encouraging competing languages) is fundamentally different from Jeffersonian pluralism (diversity of values and opinions).
claims.source_passage: In considering bilingualism in America, we should therefore understand that well-meaning linguistic pluralism, which would encourage rather than discourage competing languages within our borders, is much different from Jeffersonian pluralism, which has encouraged a diversity of traditions, values, and opinions.

claims.text: The preservation of the Union in the Civil War does not guarantee its continued existence if cultural and educational vigilance is neglected.
claims.source_passage: Defenders of multilingualism should not assume that our Union has been preserved once and for all by the Civil War, and that we can afford to disdain the cultural and educational vigilance exercised by other modern nations.

claims.text: Modern linguistic pluralism, which encourages competing languages within national borders, is fundamentally different from Jeffersonian pluralism.
claims.source_passage: pluralism, which would encourage rather than discourage competing languages within our borders, is much different from Jeffersonian pluralism, which has encouraged a diversity of traditions, values, and opinions.

claims.text: The toleration of diversity is a foundational principle of American society.
claims.source_passage: Toleration of diversity is at the root of our society

claims.text: The encouragement of multilingualism within the United States is contrary to American traditions.
claims.source_passage: encouragement of multilingualism is contrary to our traditions

claims.text: Defenders of multilingualism mistakenly assume that the preservation of the American Union is guaranteed by the outcome of the Civil War.
claims.source_passage: Defenders of multilingualism should not assume that our Union has been preserved once and for all by the Civil War

claims.text: The United States must exercise the same cultural and educational vigilance as other modern nations to maintain its cohesion.
claims.source_passage: we can [not] afford to disdain the cultural and educational vigilance exercised by other modern nations.

claims.text: National literacy is essential for creating and sustaining modern civilization.
claims.source_passage: the role of national literacy in creating and sustaining modern civilization.

claims.text: Well-meaning bilingual programs can unintentionally create serious barriers to the acquisition of cultural literacy among youth.
claims.source_passage: I know that well-meaning bilingualism could unwittingly erect serious barriers to cultural literacy among our young people

claims.text: Barriers to cultural literacy result in barriers to achieving universal literacy at a mature level.
claims.source_passage: and therefore create serious barriers to universal literacy at a mature level.

claims.text: In an ideal world, all Americans would be proficient in multiple languages and cultures.
claims.source_passage: In the best of worlds, all Americans would be multiliterate.

claims.text: The primary educational step for Americans must be achieving literacy in the national language and culture before pursuing multiliteracy.
claims.source_passage: But surely the first step in that direction must be for all of us to become literate in our own national language and culture.

claims.text: The perception that standardized national linguistic borders are 'natural' is a linguistic misconception, as these borders and languages are consciously constructed political artifices.
claims.source_passage: We have come to accept such arrangements as being natural, but from a linguistic point of view they are not. French and Italian, as well as English and all the other national languages, were just as consciously and politically constructed as the national borders that separate them.

claims.text: The natural law of constant change inherent to oral languages is effectively 'amended' or overridden by the development of national written languages and education systems.
claims.source_passage: The natural law of oral languages is constant change, but that law has been amended by the development of national written languages sustained by national systems of education.

claims.text: The underlying causes of social and cultural unification were often obscure even to the historical societies that were actively producing those effects.
claims.source_passage: To be sure, large-scale social and cultural unification based on general education and a standard written language were effects whose causes were sometimes obscure to the societies that produced them.

claims.text: Nationalism is fundamentally distinct from its own self-perception; the cultures it claims to defend are often its own modern inventions or radical modifications.
claims.source_passage: Nationalism is not what it seems, and above all, not what it seems to itself. The cultures it claims to defend and revive are often its own inventions, or are modified out of all recognition.

claims.text: Universal military service is a necessary functional partner to centralized education in enabling a nation like Switzerland to achieve multiliteracy.
claims.source_passage: Switzerland has achieved multiliteracy (as distinct from multilingualism) through a small, intensive, centralized educational system that, coupled with universal military service, enables the Swiss to communicate with one another despite their linguistic handicaps.

claims.text: Certain national stories, such as the winter at Valley Forge, possess greater historical and social significance for national life than popular legends like the cherry tree.
claims.source_passage: We have more significant stories about Washington — for instance, the winter at Valley Forge — and more important traditions of national life. But the story of the cherry tree is a useful illustration of the way our national culture was formed.

claims.text: The move from oral dialects to written 'grapholects' requires the invention of new vocabulary and linguistic conventions for sustaining long sentences because oral speech lacks these tools.
claims.source_passage: Oral dialects have no settled conventions for sustaining long sentences, so these have to be invented. Oral dialects lack the vocabulary needed to serve the new economic, scientific, and administrative functions of the nation.

evidence.description: The abrupt transition from French-speaking children to Italian-speaking children across the border between Menton and Ventimiglia.
Historical dialect maps of the 14th century showing isoglosses of mutual unintelligibility between neighboring villages.
Henry Sweet's 1870s prediction that British, Australian, and American English would become mutually incomprehensible within 100 years.
Alexander Pope's 18th-century poetic observation that English changed so rapidly that Dryden would become as unreadable as Chaucer.
Historical prediction by Henry Sweet that English would diverge into mutually unintelligible dialects within a century.
The fact that modern American schoolchildren can still read Gulliver's Travels by Jonathan Swift (18th century).
The persistence of mutual intelligibility between modern British, Australian, and American English speakers.
Ernest Gellner's analysis in 'Nations and Nationalism' regarding the link between industrialization and standardized education.
The historical transition of power sources (water wheel to steam to combustion) requiring constant worker adaptation.
Ernest Gellner's analysis of the functional prerequisites of industrial society and the necessity of context-free communication.
Historical needs of textile manufacturers and government bureaucrats for tax and army raising necessitating written communication.
The presence of international core lexicon terms (history, geography, science) in all modern national educational systems.
Shared cultural references across English-speaking nations such as Achilles, Scrooge, Falstaff, and Cinderella.
The requirement for Britons to know about cricket and the Corn Laws vs. Americans knowing baseball and the Bill of Rights.
The historical case of China: having printing and stable script by the 17th century but remaining polyglot with mutually unintelligible dialects.
China's economic and industrial struggles in the early 20th century due to a lack of language standardization.
Common standards of pronunciation and spelling shared by schoolteachers in Australia, Britain, and America.
Modern Japan's successful standardization of spoken language through its national education system despite using a non-alphabetic script.
China's recent state-led initiative to adopt Mandarin and western alphabetic script (Pinyin) for standardization.
The 1635 royal letters patent issued to the French Academy instructing them to create rules for the language.
The founding and dictionary-making process of the Real Academia de la Lengua in 18th-century Spain.
The publication and influence of Samuel Johnson's 1755 Dictionary on English usage.
Comparison of spelling in the Declaration of Independence (e.g., 'public', 'honor') to modern standards to show linguistic stability.
The 1795 scientific meeting in Paris to establish the length of the standard meter bar.
Noah Webster's 1806 dictionary which introduced minor spelling changes but maintained Johnson's base.
Comparison between English verb endings (where 's' is redundant due to pronouns) and Italian (where verb endings are necessary to identify the subject).
The independent evolution of the 'he run' and 'I be' patterns in isolated oral dialects across both Britain and America.
The history of the word 'doubt', which never had a 'b' sound in English but was spelled with one to show a connection to the Latin 'dubito'.
Expert testimony from linguist Otto Jespersen regarding the rationality of regularized syntactic development.
Expert testimony from M. M. Guxman regarding the necessity of conscious normalization in the history of written languages like Italian and French.
Historical normalization of languages in 16th and 17th-century Italy and France.
Guxman's study of the history of various languages showing written norms differ from regional dialects.
Functional differences between oral dialects and 'grapholects' in anonymous communication.
Gellner's observation of the use of 'peasant dresses' at the Budapest Opera as an example of invented national high culture.
The observation that aristocratic ladies at the Budapest Opera wore peasant dresses to symbolize national identity.
The selection of Betsy Ross as a legendary figure over other potential candidates like Martha Crabtree or Sarah Smith.
The Napoleonic French education system where every child read the same textbook page on the same day.
Hugh Blair's 'Lectures on Rhetoric and Belles Lettres' went through 130 editions between 1783 and 1911.
The absence of any Scottish poets in Hugh Blair's textbook index, despite his Scottish heritage, to prioritize English national culture.
The index of Blair's Rhetoric contains names like Achilles, Aristotle, Bacon, and Shakespeare, showing the overlap between historical and modern cultural literacy.
Blair's specific analysis of Achilles (challenging Horace's view) demonstrates how he taught the 'lore' and traditional attitudes toward classical figures.
Ruth Miller Elson's study of 19th-century American school materials, showing unanimity of values.
evidence.source_reference: Ruth Miller Elson, Guardians of Tradition: American Schoolbooks of the Nineteenth Century (1964)

evidence.description: The sales figures and contents of Noah Webster's spellers and dictionaries.
Analysis of 19th-century schoolbooks showing high levels of uniformity and nationalistic content.
evidence.source_reference: Elson, Ruth Miller, Guardians of Tradition: American Schoolbooks of the Nineteenth Century (1964)
evidence.source_passage: In fact, as Elson has shown, the contents of American schoolbooks of the nineteenth century were so similar and interchangeable that their creators might seem to have participated in a conspiracy to indoctrinate young Americans

evidence.description: Mason Weems's personal letters describing his commercial motivations and his view of Washington as a pre-existing part of American lore.
evidence.source_reference: Weems, Mason Locke, Letters of Mason Locke Weems, 1784-1825
evidence.source_passage: His letters suggest that he was not consciously helping to form part of the tradition needed to weld Americans into a nation. He assumed that George Washington had already become part of American lore

evidence.description: The specific inclusion and modification of the cherry tree story in the McGuffey Second Eclectic Reader.
evidence.source_reference: McGuffey, William Holmes, Second Eclectic Reader
evidence.source_passage: McGuffey included a sterner version of the cherry-tree episode in his Second Eclectic Reader and thus assured it a place in many other readers, and in our permanent lore.

evidence.description: Abraham Lincoln's own account of his education in his autobiography.
evidence.source_passage: Abraham Lincoln relates in his Autobiography how he educated himself by carefully reading and rereading a few books . . . Weems's Life of Washington, the Bible, Robinson Crusoe, Pilgrim's Progress...

evidence.description: The specific list of books Lincoln used to educate himself on the frontier.
The inclusion of Weems's cherry-tree story in McGuffey's widely used school readers.
The linguistic shortening of 'television' to 'TV' and 'telephone' to 'phone'.
The continued readability and legal currency of the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution over 200 years.
Historical contrast between the US language inheritance and the violent impositions of national languages in France, Spain, Britain, and Russia.
The current readability and relevance of 18th-century documents like the Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution.
Historical battles (Flodden, Worcester, Drogheda) cited as the violent means through which the English language was standardized long before the U.S. existed.
The 'war of the dictionaries' between Noah Webster and Joseph Worcester regarding American vs. British spelling and pronunciation.
The Swiss model of 'multiliteracy' requiring a centralized education system and universal military service.
The 'unencouraging' examples of linguistic strife in Belgium and Canada.
China's efforts to overcome fragmentation by standardizing a national language.
Reference to other modern nations as models for cultural and educational vigilance to maintain national unity.
Reference to the Civil War as an event that preserved the Union but did not eliminate the need for ongoing vigilance.

concepts.author_definition: Seventeenth- to nineteenth-century figures, often government-directed, who fixed national languages into their standardized forms.
The relationship between national borders and national languages, where both are man-made constructs that reinforce one another.
Lines on a dialect map marking off domains of mutual unintelligibility between speakers.
A social and economic organization characterized by large-scale communication, specialized division of labor, and a requirement for a standardized linguistic medium.
A standardized form of education that provides workers with general skills and a common culture, allowing them to be retrained from one specialization to another.
Written, impersonal messages intended for a wide, unspecified audience ('to-whom-it-may-concern') that require a shared and standardized linguistic medium to be understood.
A shared and uniform system of speech and script that allows for context-free, impersonal communication across a distance.
A formal culture taught through the education system, as opposed to folk culture transmitted locally; it is necessary for dignity and usability in industrial society.
A core lexicon of modern education (science, history, geography) known by literate people globally, regardless of their native language.
Cultural knowledge shared by all speakers of a specific language (e.g., English) across different nations.
The specific set of cultural information and terms unique to a particular country that its citizens must know to be literate.
A semiphonetic writing system that connects writing to speaking through stable spellings representing specific sounds, serving to stabilize pronunciation across a population.
A deliberate construct, usually fixed by conscious planning, dictionary-making, and state-enforced education to eliminate dialectal diversity.
The ideology or metaphor under which the historical process of language standardization and dialect elimination was carried out.

The principle that the specific linguistic or physical standards chosen (like the length of a meter or a spelling) are initially arbitrary, as utility arises from the commonality of the choice rather than its inherent nature.
The deliberate, non-natural activity of theoreticians, academies, and societies to codify rules for a written language, distinguishing it from the unconscious evolution of oral dialects.
An unconsciously evolving form of language that tends toward greater rationality, simplicity, and efficiency over time.
A standardized, fixed written code that is 'essentially different' from oral speech and relies on arbitrary conventions for the sake of stability and broad recognition.
The conscious, theoretical process of establishing and fixing rules for grammar, spelling, pronunciation, and usage for a written language.
A written language standard (standard language) adapted to anonymous, distanced communication rather than face-to-face oral interaction.
A literate, specialist-transmitted culture constructed by nation-builders that reinterprets folk materials to create a unified national identity.
The specific tradition and shared lore (attitudes and associations) that an individual needs to know to read and write effectively in a national language.
The commonly shared information and traditional ranges of views regarding historical and mythical figures that aspiring readers and speakers must remember.
The specific mythology, values, and national loyalty transmitted through schoolbooks to create a unified citizenry.
A relatable, humanized version of a mythic or national figure whose private virtues serve as a moral and patriotic model for ordinary citizens and youth.
concepts.common_alternative: A 'demigod' or distant statesman whose life is viewed solely through official achievements.
concepts.source_passage: Weems deduced that the public needed a domesticated Everyman whose life would serve as a model for American youth.

concepts.author_definition: Emotional and social themes (like parent-child dynamics or truth-telling) that appeal across generations and cultures, granting stories longevity independent of political design.
A collection of shared stories and myths, often humanized and taught via schoolbooks, that weld a diverse population into a unified nation.
concepts.source_passage: some of the most persistent elements of our national lore owe their longevity to human universality rather than conscious political design

concepts.author_definition: The traditional scheme of American education that transmits shared national ideals and values.
The natural tendency of languages to evolve by shortening words that are used frequently.
Compendia of knowledge (like dictionaries or cultural literacy indexes) that democratize access to power by providing outsiders the shared codes of the mainstream.
concepts.common_alternative: Some might see standardized dictionaries as conservative or restrictive tools.

concepts.author_definition: The state of being literate in multiple languages, achieved through intensive, centralized educational and societal systems (e.g., Switzerland).
A social framework that encourages and tolerates a diversity of traditions, values, and opinions while maintaining a shared core (implicitly linguistic/cultural).
The policy of encouraging or allowing multiple competing languages to flourish within a single nation's borders, which Hirsch views as a threat to national unity.
The ability to be literate in multiple languages and their corresponding cultures; viewed as a secondary ideal to national literacy.
An internalized linguistic standard that allows speakers to maintain language stability despite geographic isolation.
An 18th-century linguistic term used by Daniel Defoe and Jonathan Swift meaning to stabilize or fix the rules and usage of a national language.
The social context of written communication (grapholects) where the writer and reader are unknown to each other, requiring standardized conventions different from face-to-face oral dialects.
The specific historical shred, patch, or cultural invention that nationalism happens to select as its representative embodiment.

cases.name: Menton and Ventimiglia
cases.description: Two neighboring towns on the French-Italian border. Despite their close proximity, children in Menton speak French while children in Ventimiglia speak Italian, demonstrating that the 'natural' language of a child is determined by a politically constructed border rather than inherent geography.
key_passages: If you drive in the French Riviera and stop at the town of Menton, you can find small children speaking rapidly in excellent French... If you then drive east from Menton for just a few minutes and pass over a line painted across the road, you will come to the town of Ventimiglia. There you can find small children speaking charming, enviable Italian.

cases.name: Fourteenth-Century Europe
cases.description: A period characterized by linguistic diversity where only closely neighboring dialects were intelligible, and significant linguistic shifts occurred over short timeframes (e.g., 100 years).
key_passages: In the Middle Ages it often happened that only closely neighboring dialects were dependably intelligible to one another. If you traveled four villages away instead of three you might not be able to understand what people were saying.

cases.name: Agrarian Societies
cases.description: Pre-industrial societies where economic units were local and fixed, allowing for mutual unintelligibility between regional dialects without causing social or economic collapse.
key_passages: In earlier, agrarian times, when economies were local, one's occupation and position in the community were fixed. Because the economic unit was confined to a small area, an oral dialect sufficed

cases.name: China's linguistic development
cases.description: China developed printing and a stable written script by the 17th century. However, it failed to standardize its oral language, remaining a nation of mutually unintelligible dialects. This lack of unification is framed as a cautionary tale for modern populous nations.
key_passages: By the seventeenth century, China had printing presses and a stable written language. But even after the arrival of printing, the oral Chinese language did not become standardized throughout the country. To this very day, China is a polyglot nation of mutually unintelligible dialects.

cases.name: China pre-modernization
cases.description: A populous nation that possessed printing and stable writing by the 17th century but failed to standardize oral language, resulting in mutually unintelligible dialects and economic difficulty in the early 20th century.
key_passages: Even after the arrival of printing, the oral Chinese language did not become standardized throughout the country.
China presented a cautionary picture of what can happen in the modern world to a populous nation that lacks language unification and standardization.

cases.name: Modern Japan
cases.description: A case demonstrating that spoken language standardization can be achieved through a national education system even without the aid of an alphabetic writing system.
key_passages: the example of modern Japan shows that effective standardization of the spoken language is possible without alphabetic script, as long as a national system of education enforces common standards

cases.name: The French Academy (1635)
cases.description: An institutional entity created by royal decree to 'purify' the French language, but whose actual historical role was to manufacture a standardized grammar and spelling to unify the nation.
key_passages: In France the process began as early as 1635, when the king issued royal letters patent to the French Academy
the real job of the French Academy... was to fix the usage, grammar, and spelling of the national language.

cases.name: Spanish Language Standardization
cases.description: The deliberate process in the early 18th century where the Spanish Academy (Real Academia de la Lengua), under royal authority, selected court Castilian as the standard dialect and published a dictionary to distinguish 'correct' words from 'low' or 'barbarous' ones.
key_passages: the language now spoken by Hispanics in our country was developed by exactly this same deliberate process.
the newly formed Spanish Academy, under the duke of d'Escalona, was given the task of purifying and improving the Spanish language under royal authority.

cases.name: English Language Standardization
cases.description: A less centralized but equally effective process involving 17th and 18th-century intellectuals and the authoritative influence of Samuel Johnson's 1755 dictionary, resulting in long-term linguistic stability.
key_passages: England did not establish a national academy, prominent intellectuals... agitated to purify and ascertain the language in dictionaries and grammars.
The famous authoritative dictionary issued by Samuel Johnson in 1755 was the most important member of this series.

cases.name: Standard Written English normalization
cases.description: The historical process where English grammar and spelling were fixed, often arbitrarily or pedantically (e.g., adding Latinate spellings or retaining redundant verb endings), creating a stable national code.
key_passages: The decision was made by those who fixed our grammar at a certain stage of its evolution, and their decision will probably stand forever.
Before Samuel Johnson's day, the first language normalizers of Britain were not content merely to fix certain illogical patterns of grammar, they were also illogical pedants in the matter of spelling

cases.name: 16th and 17th Century Italy and France
cases.description: Historical examples cited via Guxman where language normalization was carried out by a narrow social stratum to form a new type of written language through theoretical codification.
key_passages: The normalization of the language in 16th- and 17th-century Italy or France was of interest undoubtedly, to a relatively narrow social stratum.

cases.name: Budapest Opera peasant dresses
cases.description: An example cited from Gellner where members of the high-society elite at the opera wore costumes 'claimed to be' peasant dresses, illustrating how nationalism invents or adopts 'folk' symbols for its own high-culture purposes.
key_passages: But it was the great ladies at the Budapest Opera who really went to town in peasant dresses, or dresses claimed to be such.

cases.name: The Rise of Hugh Blair's Rhetoric
cases.description: In 1762, the first professorship of English was established in Scotland, not England, to teach English national culture to 'provincials.' Hugh Blair's lectures were codified into a textbook that became globally influential, defining cultural literacy for the English-speaking world and persisting in US curricula long after the Civil War.
key_passages: In 1762... the first professorship of English was established... in Scotland — where instruction in English national culture was felt to be needed.
Blair's book became one of the most influential textbooks ever issued in Great Britain or the United States.
He gathered and codified for the Scots materials that literate Englishmen had absorbed through the pores.

cases.name: American Revolutionary Legend Building
cases.description: The author describes how the US national culture was populated with specific figures like Abraham Lincoln and Betsy Ross. He notes that while Lincoln's prominence was likely, figures like Betsy Ross were selected over other equally viable historical candidates to fill roles in the national mythos.
key_passages: Abraham Lincoln was certain to become a central figure in our culture, but Betsy Ross was not.
The candidates for her position of legendary Revolutionary War heroine were potentially many.

cases.name: 19th-Century American Schoolbooks
cases.description: Textbooks from 1790 to 1900 that replaced British materials to transmit American-specific values like thrift, honesty, and national perfection, while contrasting them with European decadence.
key_passages: their creators might seem to have participated in a conspiracy to indoctrinate young Americans with commonly shared attitudes
they unanimously stressed love of country, love of God, obedience to parents, thrift, honesty, and hard work

cases.name: The Cherry Tree Legend
cases.description: The story created by Mason Weems featuring a young George Washington admitting to cutting down his father's cherry tree because he 'cannot tell a lie.' Though discredited by historians as a factual event, the story became a permanent part of American culture after being picked up by textbook compilers like McGuffey.
key_passages: George, said his father, do you know who killed that beautiful little cherry-tree yonder in the garden? ... J can't tell a lie, Pa; you know I can't tell a lie. I did cut it with my hatchet.
If this legend had been left to languish in Weems's book, it might have been forgotten... But with a sure instinct, the compilers of textbooks took up the Weems stories.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which the 'market opportunity' was truly the primary driver versus a semi-conscious desire to promote republican virtue.

cases.name: Lincoln's Frontier Education
cases.description: Abraham Lincoln's self-reported list of books (the Bible, Weems, Franklin, etc.) which he read repeatedly in a frontier setting. This limited but shared curriculum became a central myth of American self-improvement and national character.
key_passages: This typical frontier education itself became part of American national mythology after Lincoln's assassination.

cases.name: Lincoln of Folklore
cases.description: The idealized version of Abraham Lincoln who educated himself through a few core books, serving as a personification of the American educational ideal and a vehicle for transmitting national values.
key_passages: In the Lincoln story, as narrated inside and outside the school, Americans continued an ideal of a peculiarly American education, in which the reading of a few central books could yield virtue, patriotism, and prudence.
The Lincoln of folklore embodied what ordinary inarticulate Americans cherished as ideals.

cases.name: United States 1776 Linguistic Inheritance
cases.description: The historical situation where the newly formed United States inherited a stable, standardized version of English, allowing for political stability and long-term readability of its founding documents without the social strife seen in Europe.
key_passages: Fortunately, we inherited a standard written language that by 1776 had become normalized in grammar, spelling, and pronunciation.
Our ancient charters, the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution, were written in a language that is current more than two hundred years later

cases.name: The War of the Dictionaries
cases.description: A linguistic debate in the early U.S. between Joseph Worcester (who favored British standards) and Noah Webster (who favored an American flavor for spelling and pronunciation). Webster's version eventually prevailed across most of the country, except at conservative institutions like Harvard and UVA.
key_passages: Our only experience of civil strife over the language was a tempest in a teapot called the war of the dictionaries.

Worcester, the conservative, wanted to follow British practice in everything. Webster, representing the spirit of national pride and independence, wanted to introduce an American flavor into pronunciation and spelling.

cases.name: Switzerland
cases.description: A small nation that manages multiple languages through a unique combination of high-intensity centralized schooling and universal military service, following centuries of bloody conflict between cantons.
key_passages: Defenders of linguistic pluralism invite us to look to Switzerland, not realizing that Switzerland has achieved multiliteracy (as distinct from multilingualism) through a small, intensive, centralized educational system
they have achieved this only after hundreds of years of bloody conflict between the Swiss cantons.

cases.name: The American Civil War
